name: Caesium
name_origin: From Latin 'caesius' meaning sky-blue, after the bright blue lines in its emission spectrum.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Relatively rare in Earth's crust (~3 ppm); primarily obtained from the mineral pollucite found in pegmatites, with major deposits at Bernic Lake, Manitoba, Canada.

compounds.name: Caesium chloride
compounds.summary: An ionic salt used in molecular biology for density gradient centrifugation of nucleic acids.

compounds.name: Caesium hydroxide
compounds.summary: One of the strongest known bases, highly corrosive and hygroscopic, used as an electrolyte in some batteries.

compounds.name: Caesium carbonate
compounds.summary: A mild base widely used in organic synthesis for selective deprotonation reactions.

compounds.name: Caesium fluoride
compounds.summary: Source of fluoride ion used as a desilylation agent and reagent in organofluorine chemistry.

uses: Atomic clocks (Cs-133 hyperfine transition defines the SI second)
Drilling fluids in petroleum exploration (caesium formate brines)
Vacuum tube getter to remove residual gases
Specialty optical glass and infrared lamps
reactivity_summary: Extremely reactive alkali metal that ignites spontaneously in air and reacts explosively with water to produce caesium hydroxide and hydrogen gas. Must be stored under inert atmosphere or in dry mineral oil.
isotopes_summary: Caesium has one stable isotope, Cs-133, which is the basis of the SI definition of the second. Numerous radioactive isotopes are known, including Cs-137 (half-life 30.17 years), a notable nuclear fission product and radiological contaminant.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Caesium
